clean_story:
Water vapor into droplets. Heat’s invisible escape.

The word condensation comes from Latin condensare—to make dense. It’s what happens when warm, wandering water molecules slow down, huddle together, and turn back into liquid, like a crowd of runners stopping to form a circle.

Here’s the unseen labor: Warm air holds water vapor like a sponge holds ink—loose, spread out. Cool it, and the sponge squeezes. Molecules lose energy, stick to surfaces, or cling to dust specks in the air. A cold soda can sweats because the air near it chills, forcing vapor to surrender its gas form. Clouds appear when rising warm air expands and cools, trapping moisture into mist.

Precision matters. At exactly one hundred percent humidity, air can’t hold another drop. Below freezing, vapor skips liquid entirely, frosting glass like sugar on a cake. Engineers exploit this: power plants use condensation to recycle steam into water; dehumidifiers pull moisture from muggy rooms by cooling metal coils until droplets bead and drip.

Three related subjects are how fog forms differently, why sweat cools skin, and the physics of snowflakes.
